data.title: Coca-Cola Europacific Partners impresses Jefferies with its Q1 earnings report
data.description:
Jefferies reiterated its bullish view on Coca-Cola Europacific Partners (CCEP) after pointing to the solid start of the year displayed in the company's Q1 earnings report.

With its Q1 earnings report. Coca-Cola Europacific Partners reported revenue was up 6.7% on a reported basis to €5.0B, driven mainly by strong growth in Europe and continued momentum in zero-sugar and energy categories. Underlying demand was solid, with reported volume up 8.5%, but average daily sales volume was up a more modest 1.6% after adjusting for six extra trading days, while revenue per unit case increased 0.8% as pricing and mix continued to support the top line.  

Analyst Edward Mundy and his team view Coca-Cola Europacific (CCEP) as the “real thing” in the consumer staples sector, noting it is a well-run, consistent compounder with high barriers to entry that can deliver visible double-digit returns in a low-growth environment. Mundy noted the firm still has conviction in its forecast for 4% sales growth and 7% EBIT growth over the medium term with cash returns to shareholders. 

Within the Coke system, Coca-Cola Europacific (CCEP) is one of Jefferies' top picks. "Changes over the past decade in incidence and scale have made the system more robust and aligned; a step change in bottler execution capabilities and improving customer relationships provide further resilience," highlighted Mundy.

Jefferies has a Buy rating on CCEP.

data.title: Sherwin-Williams keeps 2026 guidance unchanged while raising raw material inflation outlook to low-to-mid single digits
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Sherwin-Williams (SHW) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Sherwin-Williams said it delivered “strong sales in a quarter characterized by heightened global uncertainty and persistent demand softness in most end markets,” while noting “sales exceeded guidance on a consolidated basis and in all 3 reportable segments” (Senior Vice President James Jaye).
  • CEO Heidi Petz framed the quarter as continued share capture, saying, “Our differentiation continues to widen the gap between Sherwin-Williams and our competitors as evidenced by our strong top line and robust new account growth across the business” (Chairman, CEO & President Heidi Petz).
  • In Paint Stores Group, Petz said PSG “grew by a mid-single-digit percentage,” with “Price/mix and volume both increased by low single-digit percentages,” and “Effectiveness of our January 1 price increase is trending slightly better than expected” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • Petz highlighted specific end-market trends: “residential repaint… returned to mid-single-digit growth,” “property maintenance was encouraging,” and “demand in new residential remained very challenging as we anticipated,” while “Protective & Marine… sales increased by double digits” and marked “the seventh straight quarter of high single-digit growth” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • In Consumer Brands, Petz said results were “driven by high-teens growth from the Suvinil acquisition,” with “Softness… in North America where sales decreased by low single digits,” while “Group sales, excluding Suvinil increased by low single digits” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • In Performance Coatings, Petz said sales growth was supported by “growth in every division and region,” while noting the demand backdrop: “demand in our underlying core business is still declining in some end markets” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • CFO Ben Meisenzahl pointed to the pricing shift as the year evolved, saying, “we have more than twice the pricing now in this new guide than what we had in the original guidance that we gave you in January,” and described the approach as “balancing price with the right volume” (Senior VP of Finance & CFO Benjamin Meisenzahl).

Outlook

  • Petz said, “Our consolidated sales and earnings guidance for the full year are unchanged,” while adding that “the Middle East conflict… has added further complexity and uncertainty in navigating the macro landscape” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • Petz introduced a more cautious demand view: “we expect to see some negative impacts on demand from recent events as the year progresses,” and reiterated the stance on end markets: “little support for meaningful recovery in most end markets” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • Petz increased the inflation view for inputs, saying, “we… increase our full year raw material inflation outlook to the range of up, low to mid-single digits,” and tied timing to the P&L: “sustained inflation… typically takes about a quarter or 2 before we begin seeing an impact” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • Petz said pricing expectations rose: “our expectation for consolidated price/mix for the year increases to the high end of our low single-digit range,” adding, “we are prepared to implement additional increases, if necessary” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).

Financial Results

  • Jaye reported “Consolidated sales grew by a high single-digit percentage,” and “Reported gross margin expanded by 90 basis points,” noting it was “inclusive of a dilutive impact from Suvinil” (Senior Vice President Jaye).
  • Jaye said “Adjusted diluted net income per share… increased by a mid-single-digit percentage,” and “adjusted EBITDA increased by a high single-digit percentage” (Senior Vice President Jaye).
  • Jaye highlighted cash and leverage, stating “Net operating cash improved by $200 million,” and “We ended the first quarter… a net debt to adjusted EBITDA ratio of 2.5x” (Senior Vice President Jaye).
  • Jaye said Sherwin-Williams returned capital with “$773 million to shareholders through share buybacks and dividends” (Senior Vice President Jaye).

Q&A

  • John McNulty, BMO: asked why pricing sounded “more surgical”; CFO Meisenzahl said pricing in the updated guide is “more than twice” January’s, with regional and end-market phasing and “plenty of time… to get additional pricing” if needed (Senior VP of Finance & CFO Meisenzahl).
  • Patrick Fischer, Goldman Sachs: asked what drove higher raw material outlook; Jaye cited “solvents and resins,” said “Propylene drives about 75% of our basket,” and referenced a forecast that propylene “maybe up 50% more through the rest of ’26” (Senior Vice President Jaye).
  • David Begleiter, Deutsche Bank: asked about removing “select commodity inflation”; Jaye said it was removed because “it’s very obvious that the commodities are moving upward” (Senior Vice President Jaye).
  • Christopher Parkinson, Wolfe: asked about growth spend and store strategy into 2027/2028; Petz said the company will be “extremely aggressive” in a “jump ball environment,” and reiterated a “disciplined process of looking at our portfolio” focused on “return on net assets employed” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • Ghansham Panjabi, Baird: asked about volume shifting to “low single-digit decline” in guidance mechanics; CFO Meisenzahl tied softer volume to inflation/demand impact and cited “the lowest level on record” consumer sentiment prints (Senior VP of Finance & CFO Meisenzahl).
  • John Roberts, Mizuho: asked about housing affordability actions; CFO Meisenzahl said the company wants “more of the supply opportunities” and is watching for land/supply steps that lower builder costs (Senior VP of Finance & CFO Meisenzahl).
  • Vincent Andrews, Morgan Stanley: asked about Consumer Brands margin and reallocations; CFO Meisenzahl said improvement came from “global supply chain efficiencies” and “no reallocation… Nothing here” (Senior VP of Finance & CFO Meisenzahl).
  • Michael Harrison, Seaport: asked if the “7%” increase conversations were complete and about mid-season moves; Petz said “all of those conversations have happened,” and “if we need to go again, we will go again” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • Gregory Melich, Evercore: asked about gross margin and Suvinil dilution; CFO Meisenzahl said “we would have been over 100 basis point improvement without Suvinil” and warned margin can be “a little bit lumpy” (Senior VP of Finance & CFO Meisenzahl).
  • Arun Viswanathan, RBC: asked if volume strength continues; Petz said, “The answer is absolutely, we see those continuing,” and cited “Emerald Symmetry” as “our best-performing interior product that we’ve ever produced” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • Kevin McCarthy, Vertical Research: asked what drove higher pricing and raw material cadence; CFO Meisenzahl said pricing included “everything… in January… [and] everything new… since then,” with raw inflation “heavier in the second half” (Senior VP of Finance & CFO Meisenzahl).
  • Joshua Spector, UBS: challenged why raw inflation isn’t higher; CFO Meisenzahl cited supplier relationships, saying procurement is “tighter than some of the others,” and pointed to “50% of our business on contracts” (Senior VP of Finance & CFO Meisenzahl).
  • Matthew DeYoe, BofA: asked how much packaging growth is tied to regulation; Petz said the EFSA BPA ban “is scheduled to take effect in Q2 of this year” and should drive conversions “into ’27” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • Charles Cerankosky, Northcoast: asked mortgages/household formations; Jaye said mortgage rates are “not expecting it to move a whole lot,” and referenced “a tremendous pent-up demand” from underbuilding (Senior Vice President Jaye).
  • Patrick Cunningham, Citi: asked about PCG volume guide and order timing; CFO Meisenzahl cited supply timing (“boats… 60 to 90 days”) and a potential “inflection point” on feedstocks affecting inflation/demand (Senior VP of Finance & CFO Meisenzahl).
  • Kevin Estok, Jefferies: asked about customer ordering behavior; Petz said “it’s probably a mix” between prudence and “confidence in the backlogs” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • Zack Pacheco, Loop: asked about pre-buying; Petz said, “We’re not seeing that in this moment, nothing material” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • Michael Sison, Wells Fargo: asked if architectural demand is “structurally impaired” and PSG guide; Petz said “I wouldn’t use the word impaired… under pressure,” and Jaye said PSG full-year guidance “remains in line in that low single digit” (Senior Vice President Jaye).
  • Sebastian Bray, Berenberg: asked about refinish and EMEA decorative; Petz tied refinish gains to combining “controlled distribution” and “waterborne technologies,” while CFO Meisenzahl said Europe had a “reporting mix impact” and core Europe growth was “mid-single-digit” excluding reclass (Chairman, CEO & President Petz; Senior VP of Finance & CFO Meisenzahl).
  • Eric Bosshard, Cleveland Research: asked about DIY divergence; Petz said the premium DIY customer “generally prefers the specialty store environment,” while value DIY relies on retail partnerships (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • Jeffrey Zekauskas, JPMorgan: asked about industrial price magnitude and propylene math; CFO Meisenzahl cited “bigger… on industrial, in APAC… EMEA,” and Jaye said the 50% figure was a “forecast,” adding “we’re not buying propylene… [but] derivatives” that “take some time to flow into our basket” (Senior VP of Finance & CFO Meisenzahl; Senior Vice President Jaye).

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly negative to pressing, with repeated challenges on inflation math and pricing adequacy, including “we have math out there that says you could see raws up 20%” and “wouldn’t that mean that your raw materials are up 38%, 37%” (Joshua Spector, UBS; Jeffrey Zekauskas, JPMorgan).
  • Management sentiment was slightly positive and confident, emphasizing agility and readiness to reprice: “we are prepared to implement additional increases, if necessary,” and “if we need to go again, we will go again” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • Versus last quarter, management kept the “share gain” and “little support for… recovery” framing, but the catalyst shifted from tariffs/soft demand to geopolitical-driven volatility, with Petz saying “What has changed is the Middle East conflict” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Current-quarter guidance language kept the full-year view steady (“unchanged”), but the input-cost framing shifted from Q4’s “raw material basket… up a low single-digit percentage… driven by tariffs along with select commodities” to Q1’s “increase… to the range of up, low to mid-single digits” tied to Middle East-driven energy/petrochemical volatility (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • Q1 commentary leaned more into targeted pricing execution and phasing, with CFO Meisenzahl describing region/end-market timing, while Q4 emphasized a broad “7% price increase effective January 1” with “Realization… in the low single-digit range” (Senior VP of Finance & CFO Meisenzahl; Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • Prepared remarks maintained the share-gain posture, but Q1 Q&A featured more direct analyst skepticism on raw inflation magnitude and competitor actions, leading management to reiterate procurement advantages and contract coverage (Senior VP of Finance & CFO Meisenzahl).

Risks and Concerns

  • Petz said geopolitical uncertainty is expected to pressure demand: “we expect to see some negative impacts on demand… as the year progresses,” and warned the situation is “highly fluid” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • Petz flagged commodity volatility, stating “cost for oil, natural gas and key petrochemical feedstocks… have inflated and remain volatile,” with P&L timing more pronounced “through the second quarter and into the second half of the year” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).
  • Management emphasized supply assurance as mitigation, with Petz saying the “first objective is certainty of supply,” and highlighting that “over 80% of our consolidated revenue is in North America” with raws “sourced in region” (Chairman, CEO & President Petz).

Final Takeaway

Sherwin-Williams management characterized Q1 as a share-gain quarter with high single-digit consolidated sales growth and continued gross margin expansion, while keeping full-year sales and earnings guidance unchanged. The key call shift was a higher raw material inflation outlook to low-to-mid single digits amid Middle East-driven volatility, paired with an expectation for price/mix at the high end of a low single-digit range and a stated willingness to take additional targeted pricing if needed, while prioritizing supply continuity and volume opportunities across segments.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Honeywell rebounds after six straight declines
data.description:
Shares of Honeywell International (HON) rose 0.19% in the afternoon trade on Tuesday to $212.46, ending a six-session losing streak.

The stock had fallen around 7.7% between April 20 and April 27, while the S&P 500, rose 0.91% over the same period.

Shares of the U.S. industrial giant have come under pressure in recent sessions amid a mix of corporate developments and earnings-related headlines. The company reported mixed first-quarter results, with an earnings beat offset by a revenue miss and a weaker-than-expected second-quarter outlook tied to Middle East-related disruptions.

At the same time, Honeywell continued to reshape its portfolio, announcing the $1.4 billion sale of its productivity solutions unit and progress on additional divestitures, alongside developments such as the confidential IPO filing of its quantum computing unit Quantinuum. 

According to Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating system, HON is rated Hold with a score of 3.11 out of 5, with a A in profitability, but offset by weaker grades of D in terms of growth and a D+ in valuation.

A recent Seeking Alpha analysis said Honeywell is continuing to streamline its operations through the divestiture of its PSS business. The analyst pointed out that “I believe its divestiture further simplifies HON’s portfolio and sharpens its focus around higher-margin, less cyclical automation and software-driven businesses.”

Turning to the Wall Street, analysts are bullish, with 15 out of 26 analysts rate the stock with a buy or higher, 11 suggest to hold and two of them rate it with a Sell or lower.

Shares have fallen about 5.2% over the past month but are up 8.5% year to date.

data.title: Commvault outlines FY2027 subscription ARR of $1.20B-$1.21B and targets $250M-$260M free cash flow
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Commvault (CVLT) Q4 fiscal 2026

Management View

  • CEO Sanjay Mirchandani framed Q4 as a momentum quarter, saying, "We had a strong finish to the fiscal year, delivering results at or above our guided metrics while continuing to build momentum across the business." (President, CEO & Director Sanjay Mirchandani). He highlighted subscription ARR of $989 million and SaaS ARR of $400 million, adding that Commvault views AI as demand-driving: "AI creates more data, more access and more risk, directly increasing demand for protection, governance and trusted recovery." (President, CEO & Director Mirchandani).
  • Mirchandani emphasized cross-sell and newer products, pointing to multiproduct adoption (48% of Commvault-managed SaaS customers using more than one offering) and identity resilience as a material contributor to growth: "Collectively, our identity resilience and data security offerings represented 33% of net new ARR in Q4." (President, CEO & Director Mirchandani).
  • CFO Gary Merrill underscored capital return and metric priorities, stating, "I'll start by discussing ARR and free cash flow, which we believe are the North Star metrics." (Chief Financial Officer Gary Merrill). He also said, "In Q4, we accelerated our stock repurchases to 3 million shares for total consideration of $259 million," bringing FY2026 repurchases to $446 million. (Chief Financial Officer Merrill).
  • Management announced leadership and reporting changes: Mirchandani said, "Gary Merrill, who's back as our CFO," and added, "We welcome him and Geoff Haydon as our new President of Customer and Field Operations." (President, CEO & Director Mirchandani). Merrill outlined a fiscal 2027 reporting recast and added, "Going forward, we will no longer disclose total ARR as the remaining perpetual maintenance stream will be less than 10% of our business." (Chief Financial Officer Merrill).

Outlook

  • Merrill guided fiscal Q1 subscription revenue of $263 million to $265 million and said this "would result in approximately $310 million of total revenues" with EBIT margins of "approximately 19%" and diluted share count of "approximately 42 million shares." (Chief Financial Officer Merrill).
  • For FY2027, Merrill guided subscription ARR growth of 18% to 19% to $1.20 billion to $1.21 billion and said SaaS ARR is expected to "exceed $0.5 billion of ARR by the end of fiscal 2027." (Chief Financial Officer Merrill). He guided subscription revenue of $1.115 billion to $1.125 billion and total revenue of $1.30 billion to $1.31 billion, with non-GAAP EBIT margins of 20.5% and free cash flow of $250 million to $260 million "weighted towards the second half of the fiscal year." (Chief Financial Officer Merrill).
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s tone on forward visibility (Q3: management said it was "not going to talk about next year right now" (Danielle Abrahamsen)), Q4 introduced explicit FY2027 growth, margin, revenue, cash flow, and share count targets and a refreshed $250 million buyback authorization. (Chief Financial Officer Merrill).

Financial Results

  • Mirchandani said Q4 subscription ARR increased 27% to $989 million and SaaS ARR grew 42% to $400 million; he also stated, "Subscription revenue grew 20% to $208 million," and "we generated a record free cash flow of $132 million in Q4." (President, CEO & Director Mirchandani).
  • Merrill reported Q4 total revenue increased 13% to $312 million and SaaS revenue grew 43% to $93 million; he added that revenue from transactions over $100,000 increased 9%. (Chief Financial Officer Merrill).
  • Merrill detailed profitability and spending, stating, "Q4 consolidated gross margin expanded 30 basis points sequentially to 81.8%" and "Non-GAAP EBIT in Q4 was $66 million, representing a non-GAAP EBIT margin of 21.3%." (Chief Financial Officer Merrill). He attributed margin improvement to "continued improvement in SaaS hosting margins driven by scale efficiencies and ongoing product optimization" and said operating expenses were $187 million. (Chief Financial Officer Merrill).

Q&A

  • Todd Weller, Stephens: asked about FY2027 sales comp changes; Chief Financial Officer Merrill said Commvault does not disclose discrete components but comp is geared to "new customer acquisition" and "cross-sell, so platform expansion in our hybrid environment."
  • Aaron Rakers, Wells Fargo: asked about Unity engagement and later about longer-term margin potential; Chief Financial Officer Merrill pointed to roughly 600 subscription customers added in the quarter and said 20.5% EBIT margin is a "baseline," adding, "there is leverage in this business model" from scaling SaaS and AI-driven efficiencies. (Chief Financial Officer Merrill).
  • Michael Romanelli, Mizuho: asked puts-and-takes behind FY2027 guidance and whether leadership changes altered assumptions; President, CEO & Director Mirchandani said the transition was synchronized with fiscal year start and "it was 2 in the box, getting it done," adding, "I feel very good actually about the timing."
  • Eric Heath, KeyBanc: pressed on macro and memory pricing impacts; Chief Financial Officer Merrill said "the current trends are baked into our guidance" and cited partnerships, helping customers "sweat the assets," and the SaaS platform as mitigations.
  • James Fish, Piper Sandler: asked if hardware costs are shifting workloads to cloud and requested cloud gross margin specifics; Chief Financial Officer Merrill said the shift is "not yet" material to SaaS and added, "I don't have the margins in front of me, but we can get them back to you," while targeting SaaS margins "well north of 70%" over the next couple of years. (Chief Financial Officer Merrill).
  • Howard Ma, Guggenheim: asked about contract duration compression; Chief Financial Officer Merrill said Q3-to-Q4 showed "roughly no change to term length" and that renewal-term expectations are modeled into FY2027.
  • Joe Vandrick, Deutsche Bank: asked about identity deal size and adoption penetration; Chief Financial Officer Merrill said Commvault does not disclose ASP, called identity "a good land or a good expand motion," and said adoption is "still a very small proportion" of the installed base.
  • Thomas Blakey, Cantor: asked for implied FY2027 net new ARR; Chief Financial Officer Merrill said FY2027 implies "roughly $190 million" of subscription net new ARR and added he will not guide quarterly net new ARR. (Chief Financial Officer Merrill).

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, with repeated pressure on hardware/memory impacts, contract duration compression, renewal behavior, and the sustainability of guidance assumptions (e.g., Howard Ma asking if guidance "appropriately bake[d] in potential contract duration compression?").
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing confidence and guidance discipline, including, "providing a number externally that we feel confident in." (Chief Financial Officer Merrill).
  • Versus Q3, Q4 carried more confidence through concrete FY2027 targets and governance changes, while Q3 featured more deferral on forward guidance and heavier focus on explaining quarterly cash-flow timing. (Chief Financial Officer Merrill; Danielle Abrahamsen).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 emphasized identity resilience monetization and multiproduct adoption as near-term drivers, while Q3 leaned more heavily on Unity/ResOps positioning, partner accolades, and early platform reception.
  • Management changes were a key Q4 difference: Merrill returned as CFO and Geoff Haydon joined as President of Customer and Field Operations; Q3 financial discussion was led by Chief Accounting Officer Danielle Abrahamsen.
  • Q4 introduced FY2027 reporting changes (recast revenue/ARR, fewer KPIs, annualized subscription NDR) and a stated plan to stop disclosing total ARR; Q3 focused on a cost optimization program initiation and Q4 cash flow normalization expectations.
  • Analyst focus shifted from Q3’s concentration on SaaS NRR math and net new ARR mix to Q4’s emphasis on memory pricing, contract duration, margin leverage, and the mechanics behind new FY2027 guidance.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged memory/component pricing and broader macro as active considerations, with Merrill saying pipeline management incorporates these trends and mitigation includes storage-partner breadth, helping customers extend existing infrastructure, and offering SaaS deployment flexibility. (Chief Financial Officer Merrill).
  • Contract duration and renewal dynamics were raised repeatedly by analysts; Merrill said expectations for renewal term length are factored into FY2027 and noted quarterly fluctuation risk due to mix and renewal timing. (Chief Financial Officer Merrill).

Final Takeaway

Commvault’s management described Q4 as a capstone to fiscal 2026 momentum, anchored by growing subscription ARR, accelerating SaaS scale, and record quarterly free cash flow, while positioning AI-driven data growth and identity threats as demand tailwinds. Entering fiscal 2027, the company set explicit targets for subscription ARR, revenue, margins, and free cash flow, paired with reporting simplification and continued buybacks, and maintained that hardware/memory volatility and contract duration variability are being managed within its hybrid-platform strategy and baked into guidance.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: TransUnion outlines 2026 organic revenue growth of 8% to 9% and adjusted EPS of $4.68 to $4.75 while adding Mexico acquisition
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: TransUnion (TRU) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "So we started the year very strong, exceeding our first quarter guidance for revenue, adjusted EBITDA and adjusted diluted earnings per share" (President, CEO & Director Christopher Cartwright), alongside "11% growth versus our 8% to 9% guidance" and "U.S. markets grew 14%" led by "Financial Services ... up 24%".
  • "In line with our disciplined M&A approach ... we recently completed 2 acquisitions" (President, CEO & Director Cartwright): "Trans Union de Mexico" and "the smaller acquisition of RealNetworks Mobile division," plus "we repurchased $25 million of shares year-to-date through April" and "expect to increase repurchases over the rest of the year."
  • "To date, we have not observed any change in customer behavior tied to these developments" (President, CEO & Director Cartwright) regarding the Iran conflict; "Through mid-April, volume and revenue trends have remained at or ahead of our expectations."
  • "We are maintaining our full year organic constant currency guidance, including revenue growth of 8% to 9%" (President, CEO & Director Cartwright), while noting the guide uplift mainly reflects Mexico: "The increase to the high end of our guidance ... primarily reflects the addition of TU de Mexico."
  • "Adjusted diluted earnings per share was $1.18" (Executive VP & CFO Todd Cello), with "total revenue increased 14% on a reported and 11% on an organic constant currency basis" and "adjusted EBITDA margin was 35.2%, down 100 basis points year-over-year."

Outlook

  • "In the second quarter, we are guiding revenue to be between $1.271 billion to $1.283 billion" (Executive VP & CFO Cello), and "we expect our adjusted diluted earnings per share to be between $1.13 and $1.15."
  • "For full year guidance, we expect revenue to be between $5.1 billion and $5.135 billion" (Executive VP & CFO Cello), "adjusted EBITDA to be $1.796 billion to $1.816 billion" and "adjusted diluted earnings per share to be $4.68 to $4.75" while keeping "our organic constant currency assumptions ... unchanged at 8% to 9%" (and "5% to 6%, excluding FICO mortgage royalties").
  • "While the first quarter exceeded expectations, we modestly lowered volume assumptions for the remainder of the year to account for recent interest rate volatility" (Executive VP & CFO Cello), and "we plan to support continued score valuation ... with free VantageScore offered ... through the end of 2026" (President, CEO & Director Cartwright).

Financial results

  • "We exceeded the high end of our guidance across all key metrics by $41 million on revenue and $18 million on adjusted EBITDA" (Executive VP & CFO Cello), and "excluding the Mexico acquisition" the revenue and EBITDA outperformance was "$22 million and $8 million."
  • "Mortgage revenue grew 50%" in Q1 (Executive VP & CFO Cello), while "excluding FICO royalties, revenue grew 24% compared to inquiries up 7%" and management cited February rate-driven activity that "was fairly short-lived" before "a March normalization" (President, CEO & Director Cartwright).
  • "We ended the first quarter with $5.6 billion of debt and $733 million of cash" (Executive VP & CFO Cello); "our leverage ratio ... increased modestly to 2.8x" after funding "the approximately $660 million purchase for TransUnion de Mexico" including "$520 million drawn from our credit revolver."

Q&A

  • Toni Kaplan, Morgan Stanley: asked about FHFA/HUD comments and potential pricing scrutiny; President, CEO & Director Cartwright said, "we're not entirely clear on what Director Pulte was referring to" and added, "we firmly believe tri-merge is the gold standard" and "we charge $10 to $12 per report."
  • Andrew Steinerman, JPMorgan: asked how India returns to longer-term double-digit growth; President, CEO & Director Cartwright said, "we think India will deliver mid-single-digit growth over 2026" and "hopefully, that gets us back to sustained low double-digit growth."
  • Andrew Nicholas, William Blair: asked about AI adoption pace; President, CEO & Director Cartwright said, "we're still in the very early innings of AI adoption" and pointed to internal "solid 30% plus productivity" for developers and "2x to 3x productivity improvements" in analytics.
  • Jeffrey Meuler, Baird: asked about Vantage LLPAs and share shift timing; President, CEO & Director Cartwright responded, "for our guidance purposes this year, we didn't assume any share shift" and "we're not sure about the time frame for public release" of the LLPA guides.
  • Faiza Alwy, Deutsche Bank: asked about non-credit growth contribution and what softening could be absorbed; Executive VP & CFO Cello highlighted "TruIQ Analytics," "alternative data" and "Trusted Call Solutions" as outperformers, while President, CEO & Director Cartwright said, "we're not seeing really any negative impact on any type of loan volumes at this point."
  • Manav Patnaik, Barclays: asked for parameters around cushion in guidance; Executive VP & CFO Cello said, "by us printing at 7% and then maintaining the organic constant currency growth rates, we end up for the full year at a 6% rate" and "the second half is 5%."
  • Ashish Sabadra, RBC: asked if Iran conflict impacts international markets; President, CEO & Director Cartwright said, "there is more exposure to rising energy prices" and cited pressure in "the Philippines" and that Asia Pacific softness also reflected "the end of the onetime analytics work ... in Hong Kong."
  • Kelsey Zhu, Autonomous: asked about VantageScore share gains and pricing models; President, CEO & Director Cartwright said, "we have very attractive pricing at roughly $1 per score with no tail" and "we're focused on the transition, and we're focused on share gain."
  • Jason Haas, Wells Fargo: asked what drove mortgage strength outside scores; Executive VP & CFO Cello said outperformance "primarily pertained to volumes" during the February rate dip, and President, CEO & Director Cartwright added prequalification volumes have been "favorable to our guidance assumptions" and the market is settling "between 2 and 3" pulls.
  • Scott Wurtzel, Wolfe: asked about Trusted Call Solutions drivers and trusted messaging timeline; President, CEO & Director Cartwright said integration/productization of RealNetworks Mobile tech should take "about a year," and Executive VP & CFO Cello said TCS was "a $27 million product ... in 2021," expected to be "$200 million" in 2026 and "$300 million" in 2028.

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to pressing on regulatory/pricing and mortgage structure, including concerns about "scrutinizing pricing" (Toni Kaplan, Morgan Stanley) and requests to quantify cushion and share-shift mechanics.
  • Management tone was slightly positive but cautious on macro: "we are maintaining ... guidance" and "not because we're seeing any negativity in our volumes" (President, CEO & Director Cartwright), while also emphasizing uncertainty from geopolitics.
  • Versus last quarter, management discussion shifted from transformation completion and Investor Day positioning to more policy-driven mortgage questions (VantageScore/tri-merge) and geopolitical uncertainty; analysts also concentrated more on FHFA/HUD implications than in Q4.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 maintained the same stated full-year organic constant currency growth range of "8% to 9%" (President, CEO & Director Cartwright), but updated high-end figures were tied to consolidating Mexico, versus Q4 where management said Mexico "is not included in guidance" (Executive VP & CFO Cello).
  • Q1 introduced a more explicit regulatory/policy narrative in mortgage, including "Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac have begun accepting VantageScore 4.0" and "$0.99 VantageScore 4.0 mortgage pricing" (President, CEO & Director Cartwright), compared with Q4 emphasis on pending LLPA publication and conservative assumptions.
  • Capital allocation messaging moved from Q4’s larger buyback cadence to Q1’s "expect to increase repurchases" (President, CEO & Director Cartwright) alongside a temporarily higher leverage ratio after the Mexico acquisition financing (Executive VP & CFO Cello).

Risks and concerns

  • "The conflict in Iran added uncertainty about inflation, interest rates and the potential impact on consumers" (President, CEO & Director Cartwright), and management said it is "maintain[ing] prudently conservative guidance" despite Q1 outperformance.
  • Mortgage policy risk centered on tri-merge debate and pricing scrutiny; management said, "we're not entirely clear on what Director Pulte was referring to" (President, CEO & Director Cartwright) and positioned tri-merge as critical to underwriting outcomes.
  • International volatility was linked to energy-price exposure and one-time contract lap impacts, with Asia Pacific down due to "lapping onetime contracts" (Executive VP & CFO Cello) and Philippines facing energy-driven stress (President, CEO & Director Cartwright).

Final takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a strong start with double-digit U.S. growth and higher profitability, while keeping the core organic growth outlook unchanged amid geopolitical and mortgage-policy uncertainty. The year’s updated financial targets were expanded mainly by consolidating TransUnion de Mexico, with leverage temporarily higher but with stated intent to prioritize deleveraging and ramp buybacks. In mortgage, management emphasized accelerating VantageScore 4.0 acceptance and defended tri-merge as foundational, while also highlighting AI-driven product velocity (including a faster fraud-model cadence) and scaling ambitions for Trusted Call Solutions toward $200 million in 2026 and $300 million in 2028.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Polymarket in talks for lifting 2022 prohibition on its U.S. customers - report
data.description:
Polymarket (POLYMARKET) is working to enable Americans to trade on its international platform, people familiar with the matter told Bloomberg News.

The prediction market has discussed lifting a 2022 prohibition on U.S.-based customers with officials at the Commodity Futures Trading Commission, some of the people who asked for anonymity to discuss non-public information reportedly said.

On January 3, 2022, the CFTC announced a civil monetary penalty of $1.4M on the New York-based Blockratize, Inc. d/b/a Polymarket, for offering off-exchange event-based binary options contracts and failure to obtain designation as a designated contract market or registration as a swap execution facility.

Additionally, the regulator levied a cease and desist order.

The 2022 CFTC settlement pushed the exchange overseas, leading to Polymarket falling behind rival Kalshi (KALSHI), the Tuesday, April 28 Bloomberg report noted.

Polymarket operates a federally regulated exchange, Polymarket U.S., for American customers, and there is uncertainty over what would happen to the U.S.-based alternative if the CFTC approves the company's plans for its main exchange, according to the report.

The U.S. exchange is reportedly still in beta mode with little trading.

The discussions that were carried out with the regulator include merging the primary exchange's operations and blockchain-based technology with the domestic exchange's licenses, some of the people said.

On the part of the CFTC, removing the prohibition requires a formal commission vote. CFTC Chairman Michael Selig will have to act, the report noted.

data.title: Northwest Bancshares outlines 2026 low- to mid-single-digit loan growth, backed by a $50M buyback authorization
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Northwest Bancshares (NWBI) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We delivered $51 million in net income for the first quarter, a record in the company's history" (President, CEO & Director Louis Torchio), while also citing "$191 million of average C&I loan growth" and noting that the bank’s nationwide commercial verticals "now represent approximately 23% of our commercial lending portfolio" (President, CEO & Director Torchio).
  • "We recorded a net interest margin of 370 basis points" and "achieved our third consecutive quarter of lower deposit costs" (President, CEO & Director Torchio), alongside expense performance where "our efficiency ratio [was] 59.4% and our adjusted efficiency ratio of 57.8%" (President, CEO & Director Torchio).
  • "Our GAAP EPS for the quarter was $0.34 per share. And on an adjusted basis, our EPS was $0.35 per share" (Chief Financial Officer Douglas Schosser), and "total revenue was $175.1 million for the first quarter" (Chief Financial Officer Schosser).

Outlook

  • "We are maintaining our previous outlook for the full year 2026" (Chief Financial Officer Douglas Schosser).
  • "We're pretty comfortable with the forward look on low to mid-single-digit loan growth for the year" (Chief Financial Officer Schosser).
  • "We would expect to have pretty stable margin performance in the low 370s" (Chief Financial Officer Schosser).

Financial Results

  • "Net interest income grew $300,000 or 0.2% quarter-over-quarter" (Chief Financial Officer Douglas Schosser) and "net interest margin improv[ed] to 370 basis points" (Chief Financial Officer Schosser), with management attributing drivers to "an increased securities portfolio yield and a decrease in our cost of deposits" (Chief Financial Officer Schosser).
  • "Noninterest income decreased by $5.2 million quarter-over-quarter, driven by a higher BOLI benefit recorded in the fourth quarter of 2025" (Chief Financial Officer Schosser).
  • "We achieved significant positive operating leverage of 560 basis points quarter-over-quarter" (Chief Financial Officer Schosser), and "pretax pre-provision net revenue... increased to $71.7 million" (Chief Financial Officer Schosser).

Q&A

  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James: Asked about loan paydowns and how they balance versus origination; Chief Financial Officer Schosser said the bank is "continuing to... work through our criticized classified asset book" and still expects "low to mid-single-digit loan growth for the year."
  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James: Pressed on why higher criticized/classified inflows would not translate into higher charge-offs; Chief Financial Officer Schosser responded, "as we sit here today, don't see that as a high probability."
  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson: Asked whether securities growth could outpace loan growth; Chief Financial Officer Schosser said purchases were "more of a little bit more precise tactical position" and "we're not anticipating growth in the overall percent of that portfolio as a percent of earning assets."
  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson: Asked if ACL stays near 1.15%; Chief Financial Officer Schosser said, "we're pretty comfortable with the 1.15% coverage" and "keeping that overall kind of 1.15%-ish is probably pretty likely."
  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson: Asked whether a refreshed buyback implies reduced M&A ambitions; Chief Financial Officer Schosser said it "should be looked at just as another tool in the capital management toolbox" and President, CEO & Director Torchio added, "the whisper around M&A has slowed."
  • Timothy Switzer, KBW: Asked about deposit competition by market/category; Chief Financial Officer Schosser said, "we continue to see a very strong competitive set for deposits" and "we're not seeing a lot of let-up in deposit competition."
  • Brian Foran, Truist: Asked whether national verticals have a target cap and whether more verticals will be added; Chief Financial Officer Schosser said, "there's not an upper limit" and President, CEO & Director Torchio said, "I don't think that we would ever be in a position where those were the primary drivers and overtake portfolio balances."
  • Manuel Navas, Piper Sandler: Asked about potential for lower net charge-off guidance after strong quarter; Chief Financial Officer Schosser said, "it's way too early... to pull back on the guide" and "we're not pulling out the range just yet."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral-to-slightly skeptical, with repeated probing on deposit competition, balance sheet positioning, CRE stabilization, and whether strong credit results could change guidance (e.g., "Is there potential for a lower guidance eventually?" from Manuel Navas, Piper Sandler).
  • Management’s tone was slightly positive and operationally confident, emphasizing stability and discipline (e.g., "we're pretty comfortable" on loan growth and reserves, and margin becoming "more of a grind" from Chief Financial Officer Schosser).
  • Versus last quarter, management’s prepared-remarks tone shifted from integration milestones toward execution and capital tools, highlighted by "we now operate with a buyback authorization of up to $50 million" (Chief Financial Officer Schosser).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company reiterated the same full-year 2026 framework introduced in Q4, with Q1 emphasizing execution under that plan: "maintaining our previous outlook for the full year 2026" (Chief Financial Officer Douglas Schosser) versus Q4’s initial full-year ranges.
  • Capital management messaging expanded: Q1 introduced "a buyback authorization of up to $50 million" (Chief Financial Officer Schosser), while Q4 centered on achieving "100% of the cost savings" in Q1.
  • Credit narrative shifted from Q4’s elevated, identified charge-off item to Q1’s lower loss experience, with management stressing conservatism by keeping the full-year net charge-off range unchanged.

Risks and Concerns

  • "We continue to see a very strong competitive set for deposits" (Chief Financial Officer Douglas Schosser), with management indicating pricing differences may arise in markets where the bank is "in a heavy acquisition campaign" (Chief Financial Officer Schosser).
  • CRE remains a stabilization focus amid runoff dynamics: "we stopped originating a lot of construction commercial construction loans" (Chief Financial Officer Schosser) and "let's focus on stabilization for now" (Chief Financial Officer Schosser).
  • Classified loans increased: "we did experience an increase in classified loans... attributed partially to two C&I borrowers" (Chief Financial Officer Schosser), while management stated, "we have no expectation that the increase would result in higher overall charge-offs" (Chief Financial Officer Schosser).

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a record-profit quarter supported by C&I momentum, deposit cost improvement, and expense discipline, while holding its full-year 2026 outlook steady. The quarter’s new stock-relevant update was the refreshed capital flexibility through a $50 million buyback authorization, paired with continued emphasis on organic growth, stabilizing CRE runoff, and maintaining a stable net interest margin profile in the low 3.70s.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: BP rises after easy Q1 beat; RBC analyst lauds 'straightforward' priorities by new CEO
data.description:
BP (BP) up 1.2% in Tuesday's trading after posting better than expected Q1 profit that topped analyst expectations, underpinned by "exceptional" oil trading performance and steady operational delivery in upstream.

The company said Q1 underlying replacement cost profit, its definition of net income, surged to $3.2B from $1.38B in the year-earlier quarter and was easily ahead of the $2.67B analyst consensus estimate.

The jump in profit hands a tailwind to new CEO Meg O'Neill as she confronts the need to repair the balance sheet and shed low-returning assets.

BP's (BP) net debt rose 14% during the quarter to $25.3B, as market turmoil also led the company to tie up cash in inventories and longer shipping routes.

The company also warned that Q2 upstream production likely will be lower than Q1 due to seasonal maintenance and ongoing Middle East disruption, while trading results should normalize following the strong Q1.

BP (BP) reported strong numbers, "clearly supported by an extremely volatile but supportive macro environment," RBC Capital analysts Biraj Borkhataria and Adnan Dhanani said in a note.

Earnings across downstream were well ahead of consensus, and it was notable that the company's Castrol lubricants business reported the highest earnings since before 2019, the analysts said.

"It seems the priorities are clearly laid out and look pretty straightforward: pay down the debt, get the house in order, ​and put the company on firmer footing," Borkhataria and Dhanani wrote. "We anticipate a material reduction in net debt into year-end."
